title: 36" Wall Cabinet, Double Door
summary: A 12"-deep, 36"-wide double-door wall cabinet built from ripped plywood, with a wall-anchored back support, adjustable shelves, and a full-overlay face frame.
description:
The back support board is deliberately the only thing this cabinet actually hangs from — it's inset at the top specifically so it lands on a wall stud, and the plan is explicit that the back panel itself is NOT structural and must never be used for hanging. Confusing the two is an easy mistake with real consequences: a cabinet screwed through its thin back panel into drywall alone will pull straight out of the wall the first time it's loaded.

Every pocket hole in the carcass gets planned before assembly, drilled facing the front edges where the face frame will hide them, so the inside of the finished cabinet shows no visible joinery at all. Shelf-pin holes go in at the same stage, before the sides are ever joined to the top and bottom — reaching in afterward to drill them accurately is far harder than doing it on a flat, unassembled panel.

tools.note: Rips the plywood into the 10-1/4" strips used for the whole carcass.

materials.name: Plywood scrap or 1x4, for back support

cutList.note: Cut to fit exactly, allowing for edge banding if used.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: Rip the 3/4" sheet into 10 1/4" strips on the table saw — a 48" sheet gives four, and the whole carcass comes out of three of them. Crosscut two sides at 30", two top and bottom pieces at 34", and two shelves at 33 3/4" ripped down to 10" deep so they clear the back panel; take the two 34" pieces off one strip first, since they are the longest and least forgiving parts. Cut the back top support 34" long from the plywood scrap or 1x4. From the 1x2 cut two face frame stiles 30" and two rails 33", and from the 1x3 cut the mid stile 27". Leave the 1/4" back panel uncut for now — its cut-list size does not match this carcass, so it gets measured and cut to the assembled box later.
materials: Plywood scrap or 1x4, for back support

steps.title: Prep the sides
steps.body: Drill shelf-pin holes on the insides of the side panels for adjustable shelves — do this now, before assembly, since it's much harder afterward. Drill 3/4" pocket holes facing the front edges for attaching the face frame later. For a taller cabinet, cut the sides taller; for a deeper cabinet, rip the plywood wider.

steps.title: Build the carcass
steps.body: Drill pocket holes facing forward along the front edges of the top and bottom, same as the sides, then drill 3/4" pocket holes at each end of both and screw them BETWEEN the two 30" sides with 1 1/4" pocket hole screws. That reading gives a carcass 30" tall and 34" plus two 3/4" sides, or 35 1/2", wide — which is what makes the 36" face frame (33" rails plus two 1 1/2" stiles) overhang 1/4" on each end, and what leaves the 33 3/4" shelves an eighth of an inch of clearance at each end. Every pocket hole stays on the outside of the cabinet; the inside should show no visible joints. Compare the diagonals before the last screws go home — a carcass out of square will not take a square face frame.

steps.title: Attach the back support
steps.body: Attach the back support inset at the top of the cabinet, with its pocket holes hidden on the back side. This board is what the cabinet hangs from — when you install it, screw through this board into a wall stud. For heavy cabinets or contents, add a second support at the bottom too.
materials: Plywood scrap or 1x4, for back support

steps.title: Attach the back panel
steps.body: Measure the assembled carcass and cut the 1/4" back to that actual size — the cut list's 25 1/2" x 30" back is far too narrow for a 35 1/2" wide cabinet, so trust the box in front of you rather than the number. Confirm the carcass is square first, because nailing a back on locks in whatever shape it is currently holding. The back panel is not structural: it only finishes the inside of the cabinet and must never be used for hanging. Glue it and run 3/4" finish nails around all four edges.

steps.title: Build the face frame
steps.body: Build the frame flat on a bench: the two 30" stiles run the full height, the two 33" rails fit between them, and the 27" mid stile fits between the rails. That is 33" plus two 1 1/2" stiles, or 36" — the width the cabinet is named for. Join it with 3/4" pocket holes, screws and glue, clamping each joint so no member sits proud of its neighbour; a step in a face frame is visible forever. Compare the diagonals before the glue grabs.

steps.title: Attach the face frame
steps.body: Mark 1/4" in from the outside face of each side — that is the overhang the 36" frame gets over the 35 1/2" carcass — and register the frame to those marks so the reveal is even at both ends. Screw it on through the forward-facing pocket holes you drilled into the sides, top and bottom before assembly, with glue on the mating edges. Work from the middle outward and clamp as you go, or the frame will creep as the screws pull.

steps.title: Add the shelves
steps.body: The shelves are 33 3/4" long against a 34" opening, an eighth of an inch of clearance at each end, which is what lets them drop in past the face frame. They are ripped 10" deep rather than the carcass's 10 1/4" so they clear the back panel. Iron or glue edge banding onto the front edge of each one before it goes in — that edge is the only plywood edge anyone sees inside the cabinet. Set them on shelf pins in the holes you drilled at the very start.

steps.title: Hang the doors
steps.body: Bore the hinge cups in the doors and mount the concealed hinges to their own template, then hang the two 17 1/2" x 29" doors on the face frame. Two doors at 17 1/2" come to 35" across a 36" frame, so there is 1" of gap to distribute between the two outer edges and the center — set one door first, then match the second to it rather than trying to split it three ways at once. Concealed hinges adjust in all three directions once mounted, so hang them loose and walk the gaps in afterward instead of relying on drilling accurately.
